critic_raw:
This experiment tests multi-task learning with joint embeddings of structured EHR and clinical notes versus logistic regression on structured features only for 30-day readmission prediction.

The most fundamental confound is that the baseline is not matched on information set. The multi-task model uses both structured EHR and clinical notes; the baseline uses only structured EHR. If the multi-task model outperforms the baseline, it could be entirely because clinical notes contain additional predictive information, not because multi-task learning or joint embeddings provide any benefit. To isolate the contribution of the multi-task architecture, there must be a single-task model trained on both modalities but without the auxiliary tasks, and a structured-only neural model to separate the effect of model class from the effect of information access. The current design cannot attribute any performance difference to multi-task learning specifically.

The auxiliary tasks (90-day readmission, length of stay, comorbidity patterns) are outputs that are correlated with but downstream of the index admission in the training set. Specifically, the 90-day readmission and comorbidity patterns are known at discharge for the training data but would not be fully observed for the most recent training examples near the end of the 18-month training window. Records near the training cutoff may have missing or right-censored auxiliary labels, creating a systematic bias where the model is penalized inconsistently during training depending on temporal position in the training set. No strategy is described for handling censored auxiliary labels.

The stratified temporal split is methodologically inconsistent. Stratifying by readmission status within each temporal window introduces a distributional artifact: the readmission rate in each temporal window will be forced to match the overall rate, which masks real seasonal or secular trends in readmission. If readmission rates declined over the 3-year period (a known hospital quality effect), stratification obscures this, potentially making the temporal distribution look more stationary than it is and inflating the generalizability of test set performance.

The clinical notes processing uses a 'bidirectional LSTM or transformer encoder within the multi-task model.' The choice between these two architectures is not pre-specified. If the architecture is selected based on validation performance, this constitutes an additional model selection step that should be explicitly documented and controlled for in the comparison.
all_issues_raised: The baseline uses structured EHR features only while the multi-task model uses both structured EHR and clinical notes. Any performance gap could be entirely explained by additional information access rather than multi-task learning or joint embeddings, since no single-task multi-modal control is included.

Auxiliary tasks (90-day readmission, comorbidity patterns) will have right-censored labels for training records near the 18-month cutoff. No strategy for handling these censored observations is described, creating asymmetric training supervision depending on temporal position in the training set.

Stratifying by readmission status within each temporal window forces the same readmission rate across train, validation, and test, masking real secular trends in readmission rates and inflating the apparent stationarity of the test distribution.

The choice between LSTM and transformer as the text encoder is left open, representing an undisclosed model selection decision. If selected by validation performance, this is an uncontrolled degree of freedom in the hypothesis test.
all_issues_adjudicated: The baseline uses structured EHR features only while the multi-task model uses both structured EHR and clinical notes. Any performance gap could be entirely explained by additional information access rather than multi-task learning or joint embeddings, since no single-task multi-modal control is included.

Auxiliary tasks (90-day readmission, comorbidity patterns) will have right-censored labels for training records near the 18-month cutoff. No strategy for handling these censored observations is described, creating asymmetric training supervision depending on temporal position in the training set.

Stratifying by readmission status within each temporal window forces the same readmission rate across train, validation, and test, masking real secular trends in readmission rates and inflating the apparent stationarity of the test distribution.

The choice between LSTM and transformer as the text encoder is left open, representing an undisclosed model selection decision. If selected by validation performance, this is an uncontrolled degree of freedom in the hypothesis test.
